title: Starter run
question: What does the line 'drop_ball()' do in the demo program?
boardHint: It drops 1 ball, but from where?
unlockConnection: You watch one run first, then clear two quick checks before the editor unlocks.
options: It launches one ball through the current input.
It launches one ball through input 1.
It skips the next visible ball.
It changes the chosen input before the launch.
successMessage: Correct. It launches one ball from the default input.
failureMessage: The starter line only launches one ball. It does not skip, reroute, or open anything.

title: Input numbers
question: Which input number is the center input?
boardHint: You can look at the pachinko board.
unlockConnection: Knowing the input numbers makes the first editable programs easier to read.
options: There is no center input.
successMessage: Correct. Input 2 is the center input.
failureMessage: The board uses 1 = Left, 2 = Center, 3 = Right.

title: Trace 'portal_side'
question: Which input does the program choose if 'portal_side = 3'?
boardHint: Look at the pachinko board to see how the inputs are ordered.
unlockConnection: An assignment can copy the visible 'portal_side' value into your own variable so you can reuse it for input choice.
options: Left input
Center input
Right input
No ball launches, because 'target' is only a variable name.
successMessage: Correct. 'target' becomes '3', so the ball launches from the right input.
failureMessage: The assignment makes 'target' copy 'portal_side'. If 'portal_side' is '3', the program chooses the right input.

title: Fix the variable name
question: How should line 1 be changed so the ball launches from the input under the portal?
boardHint: On the first line, both the variable name and the value assigned to it are wrong.
unlockConnection: The program uses 'target' inside 'choose_input(target)', so line 1 needs to give 'target' the value 'portal_side'.
options: input = portal_side
input = 1
target = portal_side
target = 3
successMessage: Correct. 'choose_input(target)' uses the variable 'target', so line 1 needs to set 'target' to 'portal_side'.
failureMessage: The next line uses the variable 'target'. That means line 1 should be 'target = portal_side'.

title: Write a portal-avoiding program
question:
Write a program that reads 'portal_side' and launches the ball from the other side.
If the portal is on the right, the ball must drop from the left. If the portal is on the left, the ball must drop from the right.
boardHint: This task needs an expression. What would the variable 'wrongSide = 4 - portal_side' represent?
unlockConnection: Board data can drive strategy: 'portal_side' gives the portal side, and '4 - portal_side' gives the other side.

cases.title: Portal left
cases.requirement: Launch the ball from the right side.

cases.title: Portal right
cases.requirement: Launch the ball from the left side.

successMessage: Correct. That code reads the visible value and uses it to flip to the opposite side.
failureMessage: Your answer should use 'portal_side' to choose the opposite input and launch exactly one ball.

title: Fix the ball-checking code
question: What should the code below look like if you want to skip only 'negative_ball' and drop the remaining balls?
boardHint: The current code skips the wrong ball type. In the fixed version, the skip branch should run only for the bad ball, and the other balls should still drop.
unlockConnection: Conditionals matter because the machine can react to the preview before the ball enters the board.
successMessage: Correct. The repaired version skips only 'negative_ball' and still launches the others.
failureMessage: The fix must compare 'next_ball' to 'negative_ball', skip that case, and still launch the other balls.

title: Fix the ball-routing code
question: What should the code below look like if you want to skip 'negative_ball', drop 'portal_ball' toward the portal, and drop 'center_ball' from input 2?
boardHint: The current code detects the negative ball, but the two remaining ball actions are swapped. Check which branch should use 'portal_side' and which one should use input 2.
unlockConnection: A full preview strategy keeps each ball type in its own branch.
successMessage: Correct. That version keeps the three visible cases separate and routes each one correctly.
failureMessage: The correct version should skip only 'negative_ball', send 'portal_ball' to 'portal_side', and send 'center_ball' to input 2.

title: Review: 'portal_side' value
question: Which input does the program choose if 'portal_side = 1'?
boardHint: Look at the pachinko board to see how the inputs are ordered.
unlockConnection: This is a spaced review of the variables topic.
options: Center, because 'choose_input(...)' defaults to the center when the value is unfamiliar.
Center, because it is the default input.
Right side, because 'portal_side' should be mirrored to the opposite side.
Left side, because 'portal_side' is passed straight into 'choose_input(...)'.
failureMessage: When 'portal_side' is '1', the ball should launch from the left input.

title: Trace the function call
question: What happens when the code reaches 'follow_portal()'?
boardHint: The code under 'def follow_portal():' does not run until the function name is called with parentheses.
unlockConnection: Functions matter because one named tactic can control repeated board behavior.
options: The value of 'portal_side' is changed before the ball drops.
The code only stores the definition and does not run the function.
Only the line 'follow_portal()' runs, but the function body is skipped.
The function body runs: it chooses 'portal_side' and drops one ball.
successMessage: Correct. 'follow_portal()' runs the function body once at that point.
failureMessage: A function call runs the function body once at that point.

title: Write 'follow_portal()'
question:
The main program already calls 'follow_portal()'.
Write only the helper function so it chooses the input above the portal and launches one ball.
boardHint: Edit the helper editor. You do not need to change the main program because it already contains 'follow_portal()'.
unlockConnection: This checkpoint asks you to build the portal-following helper yourself.

cases.title: Portal left
cases.requirement: When the portal is left, 'follow_portal()' should launch exactly one ball from input 1.

cases.title: Portal right
cases.requirement: When the portal is right, 'follow_portal()' should launch exactly one ball from input 3.

successMessage: Correct. Your helper reads 'portal_side' and launches once from the matching input.
failureMessage: Your helper should define 'follow_portal()', choose the input from 'portal_side', and launch exactly one ball.

title: Review: repair the negative-ball check
question: Which condition skips only 'negative_ball' but drops the remaining balls?
boardHint: The condition should read the variable 'next_ball'. Use '==' for comparison.
unlockConnection: This is a spaced review of the conditions topic.
options: next_ball == negative
next_ball == portal_ball
next_ball != negative_ball
next_ball == negative_ball
successMessage: Correct. 'next_ball == negative_ball' skips only the negative ball.
failureMessage: The condition should read 'next_ball' and compare it directly to 'negative_ball'.

title: Fix the negative-ball branch
question: Which version skips 'negative_ball' so the same repetition does not reach 'follow_portal()'?
boardHint: After 'skip_ball()', the current repetition should end immediately. Use the command that moves to the next repetition.
unlockConnection: This is the key loop pattern that turns one tactic into safe automation.
successMessage: Correct. 'continue' ends the current repetition after the negative ball is skipped.
failureMessage: The fixed version should end the current repetition after 'skip_ball()' so the code does not move on to 'follow_portal()'.

title: Write the repeated preview loop
question:
Write one loop that handles the next 4 balls.

On each repetition, check 'next_ball':
* 'portal_ball' - send the ball to the portal
* 'negative_ball' - skip the ball
* 'center_ball' - drop the ball from input 2
boardHint: There is no helper in this checkpoint. Write the whole strategy inside the loop. 'if / elif / else' works well because each ball type needs its own action.
unlockConnection: Now the whole portal, negative-ball, and center-ball strategy has to live inside your own loop.

cases.title: Portal, negative, center, portal
cases.requirement: Portal balls to the portal, negative skipped, center ball to input 2.

cases.title: Negative, center, center, portal
cases.requirement: Negative skipped, center balls to input 2, portal ball to the portal.

successMessage: Correct. Your loop handles the longer mixed preview directly, without leaning on a helper call.
failureMessage: Your loop should skip 'negative_ball', send every 'center_ball' to input 2, and send every 'portal_ball' to the portal directly.

title: Review: function calls
question: How many balls does this program drop?
code:
def drop_multiple_balls():
    drop_ball()
    drop_ball()
    drop_ball()

drop_multiple_balls()
drop_multiple_balls()
boardHint: One function call runs the whole function body. First count the balls from one call, then multiply by the number of calls.
unlockConnection: This is a spaced review of the functions topic.
successMessage: Correct. One call drops 3 balls, and two calls drop 6.
failureMessage: The helper drops 3 balls each time it is called. Since the main program calls it twice, the whole program drops 6 balls.

title: Trace the bonus map
question: If 'bonus_map = [0.5, 1, 5]', what multiplier does 'bonus_map[2]' read?
boardHint: Index 0 is the first item, so index 2 is the third multiplier, which belongs to input 3.
unlockConnection: Reading 'bonus_map' by index lets you target one input multiplier.
options: 2, because 'bonus_map[2]' reads the index label rather than the stored multiplier.
failureMessage: Remember that list indexing starts at 0, so index 2 is the third item.

title: Repair the best-input tracker
question: Which version checks every multiplier, remembers the strongest input, and launches that input?
code:
best_multiplier = bonus_map[0]
best_index = 0
index = 0
for multiplier in bonus_map:
    if multiplier > best_multiplier:
        best_multiplier = multiplier
        best_index = index
    index = index + 1
choose_input(best_index)
drop_ball()
boardHint: Walk through 'bonus_map' directly. Keep the best multiplier and the matching zero-based index, then add 1 before 'choose_input(...)'.
unlockConnection: Index access and direct iteration work together here: read each multiplier, remember the best one, and convert its index into input 1, 2, or 3.
options:
best_multiplier = bonus_map[0]
best_index = 0
index = 0
for multiplier in bonus_map:
    if multiplier > best_multiplier:
        best_multiplier = multiplier
        best_index = index
    index = index + 1
choose_input(best_index + 1)
drop_ball()

best_multiplier = bonus_map[0]
best_index = 0
for multiplier in range(3):
    if multiplier > best_multiplier:
        best_multiplier = multiplier
        best_index = multiplier
choose_input(best_index + 1)
drop_ball()

best_multiplier = bonus_map[0]
best_index = 0
for multiplier in bonus_map:
    if multiplier > best_multiplier:
        best_index = multiplier
choose_input(best_index + 1)
drop_ball()

best_multiplier = bonus_map[0]
best_index = 0
for multiplier in bonus_map:
    if multiplier > best_multiplier:
        best_multiplier = multiplier
        best_index = 0
choose_input(best_index + 1)
drop_ball()
successMessage: Correct. The repaired version loops directly over 'bonus_map', keeps the best multiplier, and launches the matching lane.
failureMessage: The fixed version should walk 'bonus_map', remember the strongest multiplier and its index, and then choose that input.

title: Write the weakest-input finder
question: Write one program that works for every visible case below: use 'bonus_map' to find the weakest input, then launch one ball from that input.
boardHint: Keep track of the weakest multiplier and the matching index. 'worst_index = 0' is a good start so input 1 still works when it is already the weakest. Then either compare the three slots directly or walk through 'bonus_map' with a loop, and finish with 'choose_input(worst_index + 1)'.
unlockConnection: The same map-reading skill can find any target input, not just the strongest one.

cases.title: Weakest in the middle
cases.requirement: Find the weakest multiplier and launch one ball from the middle input when the bonus map is [5, 0.5, 1].

cases.title: Weakest on the left
cases.requirement: Find the weakest multiplier and launch one ball from the left input when the bonus map is [0.5, 5, 1].

cases.title: Weakest on the right
cases.requirement: Find the weakest multiplier and launch one ball from the right input when the bonus map is [1, 5, 0.5].

successMessage: Correct. Your program finds the weakest input, remembers its index, and launches one ball from it.
failureMessage: Your answer should use 'bonus_map' to find the weakest input, remember its index, and then launch one ball from that input.
